title: Hänsel und Grethel
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, 7. Auflage (1857), KHM 15. Spelling of the 1857 edition preserved (Grethel, Thür, thun, gieng, daß).

sentences.t: Vor einem großen Walde wohnte ein armer Holzhacker mit seiner Frau und seinen zwei Kindern; das Bübchen hieß Hänsel und das Mädchen Grethel.
sentences.l: Before a great forest dwelt a poor wood-chopper with his wife and his two children; the little-boy was-called Hänsel and the girl Grethel.
sentences.i: By a great forest there lived a poor woodcutter with his wife and his two children; the little boy was called Hansel and the girl Gretel.
sentences.n: „Vor einem großen Walde wohnte ein armer Holzhacker“: because the sentence opens with the adverbial „Vor einem großen Walde“ (not the subject), the verb „wohnte“ comes second and the subject „ein armer Holzhacker“ follows it. This verb-second (V2) inversion is the backbone of German word order — you will see it constantly.

sentences.t: Er hatte wenig zu beißen und zu brechen, und einmal, als große Theuerung ins Land kam, konnte er auch das täglich Brot nicht mehr schaffen.
sentences.l: He had little to bite and to break, and once, when great dearth into-the land came, could he also the daily bread no more provide.
sentences.i: He had little to bite or to break, and once, when a great famine came over the land, he could no longer even provide their daily bread.
sentences.n: „zu beißen und zu brechen“ is a fixed old phrase for having barely anything to eat. „Theuerung“ (modern Teuerung) literally 'a becoming-dear' — a time of scarcity when food prices soar. The „th“ spelling is 1857 orthography; modern German drops the h (Teuerung, Tür, tun).

sentences.t: Wie er sich nun Abends im Bette Gedanken machte und sich vor Sorgen herum wälzte, seufzte er und sprach zu seiner Frau „was soll aus uns werden? wie können wir unsere armen Kinder ernähren, da wir für uns selbst nichts mehr haben?“
sentences.l: As he himself now of-an-evening in-the bed thoughts made and himself for worries around tossed, sighed he and spoke to his wife: “what shall out-of us become? how can we our poor children feed, since we for ourselves nothing more have?”
sentences.i: As he lay in bed that evening, turning his thoughts over and tossing about with worry, he sighed and said to his wife: “What is to become of us? How can we feed our poor children when we have nothing left even for ourselves?”
sentences.n: „sich Gedanken machen“ = to worry, ponder (literally 'to make oneself thoughts'). „Was soll aus uns werden?“ is a set idiom: 'what is to become of us?' — „werden aus“ = 'to become of'.

sentences.t: „Weißt du was, Mann,“ antwortete die Frau, „wir wollen Morgen in aller Frühe die Kinder hinaus in den Wald führen, wo er am dicksten ist: da machen wir ihnen ein Feuer an und geben jedem noch ein Stückchen Brot, dann gehen wir an unsere Arbeit und lassen sie allein.
sentences.l: “Know you what, man,” answered the wife, “we want tomorrow in all earliness the children out into the forest to-lead, where it at-the thickest is: there make we for-them a fire on and give each yet a little-piece bread, then go we to our work and leave them alone.
sentences.i: “I'll tell you what, husband,” answered the wife, “early tomorrow morning we'll take the children out into the forest, where it's thickest; there we'll light them a fire and give each of them one more little piece of bread, and then we'll go to our work and leave them alone.
sentences.n: „ein Feuer anmachen“ is a separable verb: the prefix „an“ jumps to the end („machen wir ihnen ein Feuer an“). Separable verbs are pervasive in German — the prefix parks at the end of the clause. „wo er am dicksten ist“: „er“ refers back to „der Wald“, so 'where it (the forest) is thickest'.

sentences.t: Sie finden den Weg nicht wieder nach Haus und wir sind sie los.“
sentences.l: They find the way not again to home and we are them rid.
sentences.i: They won't find their way back home again, and we'll be rid of them.”
sentences.n: „jemanden los sein“ = to be rid of someone (colloquial, still current).

sentences.t: „Nein, Frau,“ sagte der Mann, „das thue ich nicht; wie sollt ichs übers Herz bringen meine Kinder im Walde allein zu lassen, die wilden Thiere würden bald kommen und sie zerreißen.“
sentences.l: “No, wife,” said the man, “that do I not; how should I-it over-the heart bring my children in-the forest alone to leave, the wild beasts would soon come and them tear-apart.”
sentences.i: “No, wife,” said the man, “that I will not do; how could I bring myself to leave my children alone in the forest? The wild beasts would soon come and tear them apart.”
sentences.n: „es übers Herz bringen“ = to bring oneself to do something (literally 'to bring it over the heart'). „ichs“ = „ich es“, contracted. „würden … kommen“ is the conditional (would come) — the subjunctive marking a hypothetical; you'll meet „würde“ + infinitive often.

sentences.t: „O du Narr,“ sagte sie, „dann müssen wir alle viere Hungers sterben, du kannst nur die Bretter für die Särge hobelen,“ und ließ ihm keine Ruhe bis er einwilligte.
sentences.l: “Oh you fool,” said she, “then must we all four of-hunger die, you can only the boards for the coffins plane,” and left him no rest until he consented.
sentences.i: “Oh, you fool,” said she, “then all four of us will die of hunger — you may as well plane the boards for the coffins,” and she gave him no peace until he consented.
sentences.n: „Hungers sterben“ = to die of hunger; „Hungers“ is an old genitive of cause. „alle viere“ = all four (of us), an old inflected form of the numeral.

sentences.t: „Aber die armen Kinder dauern mich doch“ sagte der Mann.
sentences.l: “But the poor children grieve me though,” said the man.
sentences.i: “But I do feel sorry for the poor children,” said the man.
sentences.n: „jemanden dauern“ = to arouse pity in someone (literally 'the children grieve me' = 'I feel sorry for the children'). The person who pities is the accusative object („mich“). „doch“ here is a flavoring particle adding insistence — 'I really do feel for them.'

sentences.t: Die zwei Kinder hatten vor Hunger auch nicht einschlafen können und hatten gehört was die Stiefmutter zum Vater gesagt hatte.
sentences.l: The two children had for hunger also not fall-asleep been-able and had heard what the stepmother to-the father said had.
sentences.i: The two children, from hunger, had not been able to fall asleep either, and had heard what the stepmother said to the father.
sentences.n: „vor Hunger“: „vor“ + a feeling = 'out of / from' that feeling (from hunger). „nicht einschlafen können“ — a double infinitive at the clause end („einschlafen können“); with „hatten“ this is the past perfect of a modal, a construction you'll see repeatedly.

sentences.t: Grethel weinte bittere Thränen und sprach zu Hänsel „nun ists um uns geschehen.“
sentences.l: Grethel wept bitter tears and spoke to Hänsel: “now is-it about us happened.”
sentences.i: Gretel wept bitter tears and said to Hansel: “Now it's all over for us.”
sentences.n: „es ist um jemanden geschehen“ = it is all up with someone, they are done for (a set idiom). „ists“ = „ist es“.

sentences.t: „Still, Grethel,“ sprach Hänsel, „gräme dich nicht, ich will uns schon helfen.“
sentences.l: “Quiet, Grethel,” spoke Hänsel, “grieve yourself not, I will us surely help.”
sentences.i: “Hush, Gretel,” said Hansel, “don't fret; I'll find a way to help us.”
sentences.n: „sich grämen“ = to grieve, fret (reflexive). „schon“ here is a reassuring particle — 'surely, I'll manage it', not the time-word 'already'.

sentences.t: Und als die Alten eingeschlafen waren, stand er auf, zog sein Röcklein an, machte die Unterthüre auf und schlich sich hinaus.
sentences.l: And when the old-ones fallen-asleep were, stood he up, drew his little-coat on, made the lower-door open and crept himself out.
sentences.i: And when the old folks had fallen asleep, he got up, put on his little coat, opened the bottom door, and crept outside.
sentences.n: Three separable verbs in a row: „stand … auf“ (aufstehen, get up), „zog … an“ (anziehen, put on), „machte … auf“ (aufmachen, open). „die Alten“ = the old people, i.e. the parents. „Unterthüre“: the lower half of a stable-style split door.

sentences.t: Da schien der Mond ganz helle, und die weißen Kieselsteine, die vor dem Haus lagen, glänzten wie lauter Batzen.
sentences.l: There shone the moon quite bright, and the white pebbles, that before the house lay, gleamed like nothing-but coins.
sentences.i: The moon was shining quite bright, and the white pebbles that lay in front of the house glittered like so many coins.
sentences.n: „lauter“ = nothing but, sheer — here 'like pure/solid coins'. A „Batzen“ was a small silver coin; the image is of the pebbles glinting like scattered money.

sentences.t: Hänsel bückte sich und steckte so viel in sein Rocktäschlein, als nur hinein wollten.
sentences.l: Hänsel bent himself and stuck so many into his little-coat-pocket as only in wanted.
sentences.i: Hansel stooped down and stuffed as many into his little coat-pocket as would go in.
sentences.n: „als nur hinein wollten“ = 'as would only go in' — „wollen“ used of the pebbles almost as if they had a will ('as many as wanted to go in').

sentences.t: Dann gieng er wieder zurück, sprach zu Grethel „sei getrost, liebes Schwesterchen und schlaf nur ruhig ein, Gott wird uns nicht verlassen,“ und legte sich wieder in sein Bett.
sentences.l: Then went he again back, spoke to Grethel: “be comforted, dear little-sister, and sleep just calmly in, God will us not forsake,” and laid himself again into his bed.
sentences.i: Then he went back again, said to Gretel: “Take heart, dear little sister, and just sleep soundly; God will not forsake us,” and lay down again in his bed.
sentences.n: „gieng“ is the 1857 spelling of „ging“ (went) — you'll see this „ie“ spelling throughout (gieng, fieng, hieng). „sei getrost“ = an old, elevated 'be comforted / take heart'.

sentences.t: Als der Tag anbrach, noch ehe die Sonne aufgegangen war, kam schon die Frau und weckte die beiden Kinder, „steht auf, ihr Faullenzer, wir wollen in den Wald gehen und Holz holen.“
sentences.l: When the day broke, yet before the sun risen was, came already the woman and woke the both children: “stand up, you lazybones, we want into the forest to-go and wood to-fetch.”
sentences.i: When day broke, even before the sun had risen, the woman came and woke the two children: “Get up, you lazybones, we're going into the forest to fetch wood.”
sentences.n: „anbrechen“ (of day) = to break, dawn — separable („brach … an“; here „anbrach“ in an „als“-clause pushes the whole verb to the end). „Faullenzer“ = idler, lazybones.

sentences.t: Dann gab sie jedem ein Stückchen Brot und sprach „da habt ihr etwas für den Mittag, aber eßts nicht vorher auf, weiter kriegt ihr nichts.“
sentences.l: Then gave she each a little-piece bread and spoke: “there have you something for the midday, but eat-it not beforehand up, further get you nothing.”
sentences.i: Then she gave each of them a little piece of bread and said: “There's something for your midday meal, but don't eat it up beforehand — you'll get nothing more.”
sentences.n: „aufessen“ = to eat up (separable): „eßts … auf“, where „eßts“ = „eßt es“. „weiter … nichts“ = nothing further/more.

sentences.t: Grethel nahm das Brot unter die Schürze, weil Hänsel die Steine in der Tasche hatte.
sentences.l: Grethel took the bread under the apron, because Hänsel the stones in the pocket had.
sentences.i: Gretel took the bread under her apron, because Hansel had the stones in his pocket.
sentences.n: „weil“ (because) sends its verb to the end („hatte“), like all subordinating conjunctions (weil, daß, als, wenn, ob …). This end-position of the verb in subordinate clauses is a constant of German.

sentences.t: Danach machten sie sich alle zusammen auf den Weg nach dem Wald.
sentences.l: Thereafter made they themselves all together on the way toward the forest.
sentences.i: After that they all set out together on the way to the forest.
sentences.n: „sich auf den Weg machen“ = to set out, get going (a fixed reflexive idiom).

sentences.t: Als sie ein Weilchen gegangen waren, stand Hänsel still und guckte nach dem Haus zurück und that das wieder und immer wieder.
sentences.l: When they a little-while gone were, stood Hänsel still and looked toward the house back and did that again and ever again.
sentences.i: When they had gone a little while, Hansel stood still and looked back toward the house, and did that again and again.
sentences.n: „that“ is the 1857 spelling of „tat“ (did), from „thun“ (modern tun). „zurückgucken“ = to look back (separable): „guckte … zurück“.

sentences.t: Der Vater sprach „Hänsel, was guckst du da und bleibst zurück, hab Acht und vergiß deine Beine nicht.“
sentences.l: The father spoke: “Hänsel, what look you there and stay behind, have heed and forget your legs not.”
sentences.i: The father said: “Hansel, what are you looking at there, lagging behind? Pay attention and don't forget your legs.”
sentences.n: „Acht haben“ = to pay attention, take care. „vergiß deine Beine nicht“ is a gruff way of saying 'keep walking, use your legs'.

sentences.t: „Ach, Vater,“ sagte Hänsel, „ich sehe nach meinem weißen Kätzchen, das sitzt oben auf dem Dach und will mir Ade sagen.“
sentences.l: “Ah, father,” said Hänsel, “I look after my white kitten, that sits up on the roof and wants to-me farewell to-say.”
sentences.i: “Oh, Father,” said Hansel, “I'm looking at my white kitten; it's sitting up on the roof and wants to say goodbye to me.”
sentences.n: „nach etwas sehen“ = to look at / look toward something. „Ade“ = an old-fashioned 'farewell' (from French à Dieu). „das“ refers to „Kätzchen“ (neuter), so 'it'.

sentences.t: Die Frau sprach „Narr, das ist dein Kätzchen nicht, das ist die Morgensonne, die auf den Schornstein scheint.“
sentences.l: The woman spoke: “fool, that is your kitten not, that is the morning-sun, which on the chimney shines.”
sentences.i: The woman said: “Fool, that isn't your kitten; it's the morning sun shining on the chimney.”

sentences.t: Hänsel aber hatte nicht nach dem Kätzchen gesehen, sondern immer einen von den blanken Kieselsteinen aus seiner Tasche auf den Weg geworfen.
sentences.l: Hänsel however had not after the kitten looked, but-rather always one of the shiny pebbles out-of his pocket onto the path thrown.
sentences.i: But Hansel had not been looking at the kitten; instead he had kept throwing one of the shiny pebbles from his pocket onto the path.
sentences.n: „nicht … sondern“ = 'not … but rather' (correcting a wrong assumption). „sondern“ contrasts with a preceding negative, unlike „aber“.

sentences.t: Als sie mitten in den Wald gekommen waren, sprach der Vater „nun sammelt Holz, ihr Kinder, ich will ein Feuer anmachen, damit ihr nicht friert.“
sentences.l: When they into-the-middle into the forest come were, spoke the father: “now gather wood, you children, I will a fire kindle, so-that you not freeze.”
sentences.i: When they had come into the middle of the forest, the father said: “Now gather wood, children; I'll light a fire so you won't freeze.”
sentences.n: „damit“ (so that, in order that) is a purpose conjunction and sends its verb to the end („friert“).

sentences.t: Hänsel und Grethel trugen Reisig zusammen, einen kleinen Berg hoch.
sentences.l: Hänsel and Grethel carried brushwood together, a little mountain high.
sentences.i: Hansel and Gretel gathered brushwood together, a little mountain high.
sentences.n: „zusammentragen“ = to gather/bring together (separable): „trugen … zusammen“. „Reisig“ = dry twigs, brushwood.

sentences.t: Das Reisig ward angezündet, und als die Flamme recht hoch brannte, sagte die Frau „nun legt euch ans Feuer, ihr Kinder und ruht euch aus, wir gehen in den Wald und hauen Holz.
sentences.l: The brushwood was kindled, and when the flame right high burned, said the woman: “now lay yourselves at-the fire, you children, and rest yourselves out, we go into the forest and hew wood.
sentences.i: The brushwood was set alight, and when the flame was burning good and high, the woman said: “Now lie down by the fire, children, and rest; we're going into the forest to chop wood.
sentences.n: „ward“ is an old past tense of „werden“ (= wurde, 'was/became') used to form the passive: „ward angezündet“ = 'was kindled'. You'll see „ward“ for „wurde“ throughout. „sich ausruhen“ = to rest (separable, reflexive).

sentences.t: Wenn wir fertig sind, kommen wir wieder und holen euch ab.“
sentences.l: When we finished are, come we again and fetch you off.
sentences.i: When we're done, we'll come back and fetch you.”
sentences.n: „abholen“ = to fetch, pick up (separable): „holen … ab“.

sentences.t: Hänsel und Grethel saßen am Feuer, und als der Mittag kam, aß jedes sein Stücklein Brot.
sentences.l: Hänsel and Grethel sat at-the fire, and when the midday came, ate each its little-piece bread.
sentences.i: Hansel and Gretel sat by the fire, and when midday came, each ate its little piece of bread.
sentences.n: „jedes“ is neuter ('each one [child]'), agreeing with the neuter „Kind“ understood — so „sein“ ('its'). German treats children (Kind, Mädchen) as neuter grammatically.

sentences.t: Und weil sie die Schläge der Holzaxt hörten, so glaubten sie ihr Vater wäre in der Nähe.
sentences.l: And because they the blows of-the wood-axe heard, so believed they their father were in the nearness.
sentences.i: And because they heard the blows of the axe, they believed their father was nearby.
sentences.n: „wäre“ is the subjunctive of „sein“ (were/would be), here marking reported/believed thought: 'they believed [that] their father was near.' German uses this subjunctive for reported speech and belief — a very common pattern. The „so“ after a „weil“-clause resumes the main clause.

sentences.t: Es war aber nicht die Holzaxt, es war ein Ast, den er an einen dürren Baum gebunden hatte und den der Wind hin und her schlug.
sentences.l: It was however not the wood-axe, it was a branch, which he to a withered tree bound had and which the wind hither and thither struck.
sentences.i: But it was not the axe; it was a branch that he had tied to a dead tree and that the wind was knocking back and forth.
sentences.n: „den“ (twice) is the accusative relative pronoun ('which'), sending each verb to the end („gebunden hatte“, „schlug“). „hin und her“ = back and forth.

sentences.t: Und als sie so lange gesessen hatten, fielen ihnen die Augen vor Müdigkeit zu, und sie schliefen fest ein.
sentences.l: And when they so long sat had, fell to-them the eyes for tiredness shut, and they slept fast in.
sentences.i: And when they had sat so long, their eyes fell shut from weariness, and they fell fast asleep.
sentences.n: „die Augen fielen ihnen zu“: „zufallen“ (to fall shut) is separable, and „ihnen“ is a dative of possession — German marks the owner with the dative ('the eyes fell shut to them' = 'their eyes fell shut'). This dative-of-possession is standard where English uses a possessive. „einschlafen“ = to fall asleep.

sentences.t: Als sie endlich erwachten, war es schon finstere Nacht.
sentences.l: When they finally awoke, was it already dark night.
sentences.i: When they finally awoke, it was already dark night.

sentences.t: Grethel fieng an zu weinen und sprach „wie sollen wir nun aus dem Wald kommen!“
sentences.l: Grethel began to weep and spoke: “how shall we now out-of the forest come!”
sentences.i: Gretel began to cry and said: “How are we ever to get out of the forest now!”
sentences.n: „anfangen“ = to begin (separable): „fieng … an“ (1857 spelling of „fing an“).

sentences.t: Hänsel aber tröstete sie, „wart nur ein Weilchen, bis der Mond aufgegangen ist, dann wollen wir den Weg schon finden.“
sentences.l: Hänsel however comforted her: “wait just a little-while, until the moon risen is, then want we the way surely to-find.”
sentences.i: But Hansel comforted her: “Just wait a little while, until the moon has risen; then we'll surely find the way.”
sentences.n: „schon“ again as reassurance ('surely, we'll manage'), not 'already'.

sentences.t: Und als der volle Mond aufgestiegen war, so nahm Hänsel sein Schwesterchen an der Hand und gieng den Kieselsteinen nach, die schimmerten wie neu geschlagene Batzen und zeigten ihnen den Weg.
sentences.l: And when the full moon risen was, so took Hänsel his little-sister by the hand and went the pebbles after, which shimmered like newly struck coins and showed them the way.
sentences.i: And when the full moon had risen, Hansel took his little sister by the hand and followed the pebbles, which shimmered like newly minted coins and showed them the way.
sentences.n: „nachgehen“ = to follow, go after (separable); it takes the dative, hence „den Kieselsteinen“ (dative plural). „neu geschlagene Batzen“ = freshly struck coins (Münzen schlagen = to mint).

sentences.t: Sie giengen die ganze Nacht hindurch und kamen bei anbrechendem Tag wieder zu ihres Vaters Haus.
sentences.l: They went the whole night through and came at breaking day again to their father's house.
sentences.i: They walked the whole night through and came, as day was breaking, back to their father's house.
sentences.n: „ihres Vaters Haus“: an old, elevated genitive placed before the noun ('their father's house'). Modern German would usually say „zum Haus ihres Vaters“.

sentences.t: Sie klopften an die Thür, und als die Frau aufmachte und sah daß es Hänsel und Grethel war, sprach sie „ihr bösen Kinder, was habt ihr so lange im Walde geschlafen, wir haben geglaubt ihr wolltet gar nicht wieder kommen.“
sentences.l: They knocked on the door, and when the woman opened and saw that it Hänsel and Grethel was, spoke she: “you wicked children, what have you so long in-the forest slept, we have believed you wanted not-at-all again to-come.”
sentences.i: They knocked at the door, and when the woman opened it and saw that it was Hansel and Gretel, she said: “You wicked children, why did you sleep so long in the forest? We thought you never meant to come back at all.”
sentences.n: „Thür“ = 1857 spelling of „Tür“ (door). „daß“ = old spelling of „dass“ (that), always with ß in this text. „wolltet“ is the subjunctive-flavored past of „wollen“ in reported speech ('you wanted / meant to').

sentences.t: Der Vater aber freute sich, denn es war ihm zu Herzen gegangen daß er sie so allein zurück gelassen hatte.
sentences.l: The father however rejoiced himself, for it was to-him to heart gone that he them so alone back left had.
sentences.i: But the father was glad, for it had gone to his heart that he had left them all alone like that.
sentences.n: „sich freuen“ = to be glad (reflexive). „jemandem zu Herzen gehen“ = to touch/grieve someone deeply (dative „ihm“). „denn“ (for, because) is a coordinating conjunction — unlike „weil“, it does NOT send the verb to the end.

sentences.t: Nicht lange danach war wieder Noth in allen Ecken, und die Kinder hörten wie die Mutter Nachts im Bette zu dem Vater sprach „alles ist wieder aufgezehrt, wir haben noch einen halben Laib Brot, hernach hat das Lied ein Ende.
sentences.l: Not long thereafter was again want in all corners, and the children heard how the mother at-night in-the bed to the father spoke: “everything is again consumed, we have yet a half loaf bread, thereafter has the song an end.
sentences.i: Not long after, there was want in every corner again, and the children heard how the mother, at night in bed, said to the father: “Everything is eaten up again; we have half a loaf of bread left, and after that the song is over.
sentences.n: „Noth“ = 1857 spelling of „Not“ (need, distress). „in allen Ecken“ = 'in every corner', i.e. everywhere. „da hat das Lied ein Ende“ is a set idiom: 'then the song's over' = that's the end of it. Note the mother is now called „die Mutter“, though earlier „die Stiefmutter/die Frau“ — the Grimms wavered between stepmother and mother.

sentences.t: Die Kinder müssen fort, wir wollen sie tiefer in den Wald hineinführen, damit sie den Weg nicht wieder heraus finden; es ist sonst keine Rettung für uns.“
sentences.l: The children must away, we want them deeper into the forest in-lead, so-that they the way not again out find; it is otherwise no rescue for us.
sentences.i: The children must go; we'll lead them deeper into the forest, so they can't find their way out again — otherwise there's no saving us.”
sentences.n: „müssen fort“: with verbs of motion the modal often stands alone, the verb 'go' being understood ('the children must [go] away'). „hineinführen“ = to lead in (separable).

sentences.t: Dem Mann fiels schwer aufs Herz und er dachte „es wäre besser, daß du den letzten Bissen mit deinen Kindern theiltest.“
sentences.l: To-the man fell-it heavy on-the heart and he thought: “it were better that you the last morsel with your children shared.”
sentences.i: It weighed heavily on the man's heart, and he thought: “It would be better to share your last morsel with your children.”
sentences.n: „es fiel ihm schwer aufs Herz“: dative „dem Mann“ ('the man') plus 'it fell heavy on the heart' = it weighed on him. „fiels“ = „fiel es“. „wäre“ and „theiltest“ are subjunctives (would be / were to share) framing a wish/hypothetical.

sentences.t: Aber die Frau hörte auf nichts, was er sagte, schalt ihn und machte ihm Vorwürfe.
sentences.l: But the woman heard on nothing what he said, scolded him and made to-him reproaches.
sentences.i: But the woman listened to nothing he said; she scolded him and heaped reproaches on him.
sentences.n: „auf etwas hören“ = to listen to / heed something (separable „hören … auf“). „jemandem Vorwürfe machen“ = to reproach someone (dative „ihm“). „schalt“ = past of „schelten“ (to scold).

sentences.t: Wer A sagt muß auch B sagen, und weil er das erste Mal nachgegeben hatte, so mußte er es auch zum zweiten Mal.
sentences.l: Who A says must also B say, and because he the first time given-in had, so must he it also for-the second time.
sentences.i: Whoever says A must also say B, and because he had given in the first time, he had to the second time as well.
sentences.n: „Wer A sagt, muß auch B sagen“ is a proverb: once you start something you must see it through (in for a penny, in for a pound). „nachgeben“ = to give in, yield (separable). „muß/mußte“ = 1857 spelling of „muss/musste“.

sentences.t: Die Kinder waren aber noch wach gewesen und hatten das Gespräch mit angehört.
sentences.l: The children were however still awake been and had the conversation along-listened-to.
sentences.i: But the children had still been awake and had listened in on the conversation.
sentences.n: „mit anhören“ = to listen in on, overhear (separable; „mit“ adds the sense of being present at it): „hatten … mit angehört“.

sentences.t: Als die Alten schliefen, stand Hänsel wieder auf, wollte hinaus und Kieselsteine auflesen, wie das vorigemal, aber die Frau hatte die Thür verschlossen, und Hänsel konnte nicht heraus.
sentences.l: When the old-ones slept, stood Hänsel again up, wanted out and pebbles to-pick-up, like the previous-time, but the woman had the door locked, and Hänsel could not out.
sentences.i: When the old folks were asleep, Hansel got up again, meaning to go out and gather pebbles as he had the time before, but the woman had locked the door, and Hansel could not get out.
sentences.n: „wollte hinaus“ = 'wanted [to go] out' — the motion verb is dropped after the modal. „auflesen“ = to pick up, gather (separable). „das vorigemal“ = the previous time.

sentences.t: Aber er tröstete sein Schwesterchen und sprach „weine nicht, Grethel, und schlaf nur ruhig, der liebe Gott wird uns schon helfen.“
sentences.l: But he comforted his little-sister and spoke: “weep not, Grethel, and sleep just calmly, the dear God will us surely help.”
sentences.i: But he comforted his little sister and said: “Don't cry, Gretel, just sleep peacefully; the dear Lord will surely help us.”
sentences.n: „der liebe Gott“ = 'the dear Lord', a warm, everyday German phrase for God.

sentences.t: Am frühen Morgen kam die Frau und holte die Kinder aus dem Bette.
sentences.l: In-the early morning came the woman and fetched the children out-of the bed.
sentences.i: Early in the morning the woman came and got the children out of bed.

sentences.t: Sie erhielten ihr Stückchen Brot, das war aber noch kleiner als das vorigemal.
sentences.l: They received their little-piece bread, that was however yet smaller than the previous-time.
sentences.i: They were given their little piece of bread, but it was even smaller than the time before.

sentences.t: Auf dem Wege nach dem Wald bröckelte es Hänsel in der Tasche, stand oft still und warf ein Bröcklein auf die Erde.
sentences.l: On the way toward the forest crumbled it Hänsel in the pocket, stood often still and threw a little-crumb onto the earth.
sentences.i: On the way to the forest Hansel crumbled it in his pocket, often stood still, and threw a little crumb on the ground.
sentences.n: „bröckelte es Hänsel in der Tasche“: „Hänsel“ is a loose dative ('crumbled it for/in Hansel's pocket'). „Bröcklein“ = a tiny crumb (diminutive of Brocken).

sentences.t: „Hänsel, was stehst du und guckst dich um,“ sagte der Vater, „geh deiner Wege.“
sentences.l: “Hänsel, what stand you and look yourself around,” said the father, “go your ways.”
sentences.i: “Hansel, what are you standing and looking around for?” said the father. “Get along with you.”
sentences.n: „sich umsehen/umgucken“ = to look around (separable, reflexive). „geh deiner Wege“ = an old idiom, 'go on your way' (genitive „deiner Wege“).

sentences.t: „Ich sehe nach meinem Täubchen, das sitzt auf dem Dache und will mir Ade sagen,“ antwortete Hänsel.
sentences.l: “I look after my little-dove, that sits on the roof and wants to-me farewell to-say,” answered Hänsel.
sentences.i: “I'm looking at my little dove; it's sitting on the roof and wants to say goodbye to me,” answered Hansel.
sentences.n: „Täubchen“ = little dove (diminutive of Taube). „Dache“ has an old dative -e ending (auf dem Dache = on the roof).

sentences.t: „Narr,“ sagte die Frau, „das ist dein Täubchen nicht, das ist die Morgensonne, die auf den Schornstein oben scheint.“
sentences.l: “Fool,” said the woman, “that is your little-dove not, that is the morning-sun, which on the chimney above shines.”
sentences.i: “Fool,” said the woman, “that isn't your little dove; it's the morning sun shining up on the chimney.”

sentences.t: Hänsel aber warf nach und nach alle Bröcklein auf den Weg.
sentences.l: Hänsel however threw little-by-little all little-crumbs onto the path.
sentences.i: But Hansel little by little threw all the crumbs onto the path.
sentences.n: „nach und nach“ = gradually, little by little (fixed phrase).

sentences.t: Die Frau führte die Kinder noch tiefer in den Wald, wo sie ihr Lebtag noch nicht gewesen waren.
sentences.l: The woman led the children yet deeper into the forest, where they their life-day yet not been were.
sentences.i: The woman led the children still deeper into the forest, where they had never been in all their lives.
sentences.n: „ihr Lebtag“ = in all one's life (an old adverbial phrase, here 'never in their lives').

sentences.t: Da ward wieder ein großes Feuer angemacht, und die Mutter sagte „bleibt nur da sitzen, ihr Kinder, und wenn ihr müde seid, könnt ihr ein wenig schlafen: wir gehen in den Wald und hauen Holz, und Abends, wenn wir fertig sind, kommen wir und holen euch ab.“
sentences.l: There was again a great fire made, and the mother said: “stay just there sitting, you children, and when you tired are, can you a little sleep: we go into the forest and hew wood, and of-an-evening, when we finished are, come we and fetch you off.”
sentences.i: Again a great fire was made, and the mother said: “Just stay sitting there, children, and when you're tired you may sleep a little; we're going into the forest to chop wood, and in the evening, when we're done, we'll come and fetch you.”
sentences.n: „da sitzen bleiben“ = to remain sitting there („bleiben“ + bare infinitive „sitzen“). „ward … angemacht“ = passive with old „ward“ (= wurde).

sentences.t: Als es Mittag war, theilte Grethel ihr Brot mit Hänsel, der sein Stück auf den Weg gestreut hatte.
sentences.l: When it midday was, shared Grethel her bread with Hänsel, who his piece onto the path scattered had.
sentences.i: When it was midday, Gretel shared her bread with Hansel, who had scattered his piece along the path.
sentences.n: „theilte“ = 1857 spelling of „teilte“ (shared/divided). „der“ is a relative pronoun ('who'), sending „gestreut hatte“ to the end.

sentences.t: Dann schliefen sie ein, und der Abend vergieng, aber niemand kam zu den armen Kindern.
sentences.l: Then slept they in, and the evening passed, but no-one came to the poor children.
sentences.i: Then they fell asleep, and the evening passed, but no one came to the poor children.
sentences.n: „vergieng“ = 1857 spelling of „verging“ (passed, of time).

sentences.t: Sie erwachten erst in der finstern Nacht, und Hänsel tröstete sein Schwesterchen und sagte, „wart nur, Grethel, bis der Mond aufgeht, dann werden wir die Brotbröcklein sehen, die ich ausgestreut habe, die zeigen uns den Weg nach Haus.“
sentences.l: They awoke only in the dark night, and Hänsel comforted his little-sister and said: “wait just, Grethel, until the moon up-goes, then will we the bread-crumbs see, which I out-scattered have, they show us the way to home.”
sentences.i: They did not wake until the dark of night, and Hansel comforted his little sister and said: “Just wait, Gretel, until the moon rises; then we'll see the breadcrumbs I scattered, and they'll show us the way home.”
sentences.n: „erst“ here = 'not until, only then' (not 'first'). „aufgehen“ (of the moon/sun) = to rise; „ausstreuen“ = to scatter out. Both separable.

sentences.t: Als der Mond kam, machten sie sich auf, aber sie fanden kein Bröcklein mehr, denn die viel tausend Vögel, die im Walde und im Felde umher fliegen, die hatten sie weggepickt.
sentences.l: When the moon came, made they themselves up, but they found no little-crumb more, for the many thousand birds, which in-the forest and in-the field around fly, they had them away-pecked.
sentences.i: When the moon came, they set out, but they found not a single crumb more, for the many thousand birds that fly about in forest and field had pecked them all up.
sentences.n: „sich aufmachen“ = to set out (reflexive, separable). The resumptive „die“ before „hatten sie weggepickt“ picks up the subject („die … Vögel … die hatten“) — a colloquial doubling. „wegpicken“ = to peck away.

sentences.t: Hänsel sagte zu Grethel „wir werden den Weg schon finden,“ aber sie fanden ihn nicht.
sentences.l: Hänsel said to Grethel: “we will the way surely find,” but they found it not.
sentences.i: Hansel said to Gretel: “We'll surely find the way,” but they did not find it.

sentences.t: Sie giengen die ganze Nacht und noch einen Tag von Morgen bis Abend, aber sie kamen aus dem Wald nicht heraus, und waren so hungrig, denn sie hatten nichts als die paar Beeren, die auf der Erde standen.
sentences.l: They went the whole night and yet a day from morning until evening, but they came out-of the forest not out, and were so hungry, for they had nothing but the few berries, which on the earth stood.
sentences.i: They walked the whole night and another day, from morning till evening, but they could not get out of the forest, and were so hungry, for they had nothing but the few berries growing on the ground.
sentences.n: „herauskommen aus“ = to get out of (separable). „nichts als“ = nothing but. „die auf der Erde standen“: of plants/berries, „stehen“ = to grow/stand there.

sentences.t: Und weil sie so müde waren daß die Beine sie nicht mehr tragen wollten, so legten sie sich unter einen Baum und schliefen ein.
sentences.l: And because they so tired were that the legs them no more carry wanted, so laid they themselves under a tree and slept in.
sentences.i: And because they were so tired that their legs would no longer carry them, they lay down under a tree and fell asleep.
sentences.n: „die Beine wollten sie nicht mehr tragen“: „wollen“ of the legs, almost personified ('the legs no longer wanted to carry them'). The „so“ resumes the main clause after the long „weil … daß …“ subordinate stretch.

sentences.t: Nun wars schon der dritte Morgen, daß sie ihres Vaters Haus verlassen hatten.
sentences.l: Now was-it already the third morning that they their father's house left had.
sentences.i: Now it was already the third morning since they had left their father's house.
sentences.n: „wars“ = „war es“. „daß“ here means 'since' in the sense 'that [it was] the third morning that…'.

sentences.t: Sie fiengen wieder an zu gehen, aber sie geriethen immer tiefer in den Wald und wenn nicht bald Hilfe kam, so mußten sie verschmachten.
sentences.l: They began again to go, but they got ever deeper into the forest, and if not soon help came, so must they perish.
sentences.i: They began to walk again, but they only got deeper and deeper into the forest, and if help did not come soon, they were bound to perish.
sentences.n: „gerathen“ (modern geraten) = to end up / get (into a state or place) — often unintentionally. „geriethen“ is the 1857 spelling. „verschmachten“ = to languish, perish from hunger/thirst.

sentences.t: Als es Mittag war, sahen sie ein schönes schneeweißes Vöglein auf einem Ast sitzen, das sang so schön, daß sie stehen blieben und ihm zuhörten.
sentences.l: When it midday was, saw they a beautiful snow-white little-bird on a branch sit, that sang so beautifully that they standing stayed and to-it listened.
sentences.i: When it was midday, they saw a lovely snow-white little bird sitting on a branch, and it sang so beautifully that they stopped and listened to it.
sentences.n: „sahen … ein Vöglein sitzen“: perception verb („sehen“) + bare infinitive („sitzen“) — 'saw a bird sit(ting)'. „zuhören“ = to listen to (separable), takes the dative („ihm“). „stehen bleiben“ = to stop, halt.

sentences.t: Und als es fertig war, schwang es seine Flügel und flog vor ihnen her, und sie giengen ihm nach, bis sie zu einem Häuschen gelangten, auf dessen Dach es sich setzte, und als sie ganz nah heran kamen, so sahen sie daß das Häuslein aus Brot gebaut war, und mit Kuchen gedeckt; aber die Fenster waren von hellem Zucker.
sentences.l: And when it finished was, swung it its wings and flew before them along, and they went it after, until they to a little-house came, on whose roof it itself set, and when they quite near up came, so saw they that the little-house out-of bread built was, and with cake covered; but the windows were of bright sugar.
sentences.i: And when it had finished, it beat its wings and flew off ahead of them, and they followed it until they came to a little house, on whose roof it perched; and when they came quite close, they saw that the little house was built of bread and roofed with cake, and the windows were of clear sugar.
sentences.n: „vor ihnen her fliegen“ = to fly along ahead of them. „auf dessen Dach“: „dessen“ = 'whose' (genitive relative). „heran kommen“ = to come up close (separable).

sentences.t: „Da wollen wir uns dran machen,“ sprach Hänsel, „und eine gesegnete Mahlzeit halten.
sentences.l: “There want we ourselves to-it set,” spoke Hänsel, “and a blessed meal hold.
sentences.i: “Let's set to it,” said Hansel, “and have a blessed meal.
sentences.n: „sich an etwas machen“ = to set about / start on something. „dran“ = daran ('to it'). „eine Mahlzeit halten“ = to have a meal.

sentences.t: Ich will ein Stück vom Dach essen, Grethel, du kannst vom Fenster essen, das schmeckt süß.“
sentences.l: I will a piece of-the roof eat, Grethel, you can of-the window eat, that tastes sweet.
sentences.i: I'll eat a piece of the roof, Gretel; you can eat some of the window — that tastes sweet.”
sentences.n: „vom“ = „von dem“ ('of the'). „schmecken“ = to taste (of food); „das schmeckt süß“ = 'that tastes sweet'.

sentences.t: Hänsel reichte in die Höhe und brach sich ein wenig vom Dach ab, um zu versuchen wie es schmeckte, und Grethel stellte sich an die Scheiben und knuperte daran.
sentences.l: Hänsel reached into the height and broke himself a little off-of the roof, in-order to try how it tasted, and Grethel placed herself at the panes and nibbled thereat.
sentences.i: Hansel reached up and broke off a little of the roof to try how it tasted, and Gretel stood by the panes and nibbled at them.
sentences.n: „abbrechen“ = to break off (separable): „brach … ab“. „um zu“ + infinitive = 'in order to'. „knupern“ (dialectal) = to nibble/gnaw; „daran“ = 'at it'.

sentences.t: Da rief eine feine Stimme aus der Stube heraus
sentences.l: There called a fine voice out-of the parlor out
sentences.i: Then a thin little voice called out from inside the room:
sentences.n: „herausrufen“ = to call out (separable). „Stube“ = a (heated) living room, parlor. „fein“ of a voice = thin, delicate.

sentences.t:
„knuper, knuper, kneischen,
wer knupert an meinem Häuschen?“
sentences.l:
“nibble, nibble, gnaw-kin,
who nibbles at my little-house?”
sentences.i:
“Nibble, nibble, nibbling,
who is nibbling at my house?”
sentences.n: A nonsense-rhyme built on „knupern“ (to nibble). „kneischen“ is an invented rhyming word (roughly 'gnawing'); the sound-play, not the sense, carries it. Rendered here to keep the sing-song and the nibble image.

sentences.t: die Kinder antworteten
sentences.l: the children answered
sentences.i: the children answered:

sentences.t:
„der Wind, der Wind,
das himmlische Kind,“
sentences.l:
“the wind, the wind,
the heavenly child,”
sentences.i:
“The wind, the wind,
the heavenly child,”
sentences.n: A rhyming brush-off (Wind / Kind): 'only the wind, heaven's child' — i.e. no one, pay it no mind.

sentences.t: und aßen weiter, ohne sich irre machen zu lassen.
sentences.l: and ate further, without themselves confused make to let.
sentences.i: and went on eating, without letting themselves be put off.
sentences.n: „sich irre machen lassen“ = to let oneself be confused/put off. „ohne … zu“ + infinitive = 'without …ing'.

sentences.t: Hänsel, dem das Dach sehr gut schmeckte, riß sich ein großes Stück davon herunter, und Grethel stieß eine ganze runde Fensterscheibe heraus, setzte sich nieder, und that sich wohl damit.
sentences.l: Hänsel, to-whom the roof very well tasted, tore himself a great piece of-it down, and Grethel pushed a whole round window-pane out, set herself down, and did herself well with-it.
sentences.i: Hansel, who found the roof very much to his taste, tore off a large piece of it, and Gretel pushed out a whole round pane of window and sat down and enjoyed herself with it.
sentences.n: „dem das Dach gut schmeckte“: dative relative („dem“) — 'to whom the roof tasted good' = 'who liked the taste of the roof'. „herunterreißen“, „hinausstoßen“ both separable. „sich wohl thun“ = to enjoy oneself, indulge.

sentences.t: Da gieng auf einmal die Thüre auf, und eine steinalte Frau, die sich auf eine Krücke stützte, kam heraus geschlichen.
sentences.l: There went all-at-once the door open, and a stone-old woman, who herself on a crutch supported, came out crept.
sentences.i: Then all at once the door opened, and an ancient woman, leaning on a crutch, came creeping out.
sentences.n: „aufgehen“ (of a door) = to open; „gieng … auf“. „auf einmal“ = all at once. „steinalt“ = 'stone-old', i.e. extremely old. „kam … geschlichen“: 'came creeping' (motion verb + past participle).

sentences.t: Hänsel und Grethel erschracken so gewaltig, daß sie fallen ließen was sie in den Händen hielten.
sentences.l: Hänsel and Grethel were-frightened so mightily that they fall let what they in the hands held.
sentences.i: Hansel and Gretel were so terribly frightened that they dropped what they held in their hands.
sentences.n: „erschrecken“ (intransitive, strong: erschrak/erschracken) = to be startled, take fright. „fallen lassen“ = to drop (let fall).

sentences.t: Die Alte aber wackelte mit dem Kopfe und sprach „ei, ihr lieben Kinder, wer hat euch hierher gebracht? kommt nur herein und bleibt bei mir, es geschieht euch kein Leid.“
sentences.l: The old-one however wobbled with the head and spoke: “ei, you dear children, who has you hither brought? come just in and stay with me, it happens to-you no harm.”
sentences.i: But the old woman wagged her head and said: “Ah, you dear children, who has brought you here? Do come in and stay with me; no harm will come to you.”
sentences.n: „die Alte“ = the old woman (adjective used as noun). „mit dem Kopfe wackeln“ = to wag one's head. „euch geschieht kein Leid“ = 'no harm happens to you' (dative „euch“). „ei“ = a soft interjection ('ah, well now').

sentences.t: Sie faßte beide an der Hand und führte sie in ihr Häuschen.
sentences.l: She grasped both by the hand and led them into her little-house.
sentences.i: She took both of them by the hand and led them into her little house.

sentences.t: Da ward gutes Essen aufgetragen, Milch und Pfannekuchen mit Zucker, Äpfel und Nüsse.
sentences.l: There was good food served-up, milk and pancakes with sugar, apples and nuts.
sentences.i: Then good food was served up: milk and pancakes with sugar, apples and nuts.
sentences.n: „auftragen“ = to serve up (food), separable; „ward aufgetragen“ = passive with old „ward“. „Pfannekuchen“ = pancakes (older form of Pfannkuchen).

sentences.t: Hernach wurden zwei schöne Bettlein weiß gedeckt, und Hänsel und Grethel legten sich hinein und meinten sie wären im Himmel.
sentences.l: Thereafter were two lovely little-beds white made, and Hänsel and Grethel laid themselves in and thought they were in heaven.
sentences.i: After that two pretty little beds were made up with white linen, and Hansel and Gretel lay down in them and thought they were in heaven.
sentences.n: „weiß decken“ = to make up (a bed) with white linen. „meinten sie wären im Himmel“: „wären“ subjunctive in reported thought ('thought [that] they were in heaven').

sentences.t: Die Alte hatte sich nur so freundlich angestellt, sie war aber eine böse Hexe, die den Kindern auflauerte, und hatte das Brothäuslein bloß gebaut, um sie herbeizulocken.
sentences.l: The old-one had herself only so friendly acted, she was however an evil witch, who to-the children lay-in-wait, and had the bread-house merely built in-order them to-lure-hither.
sentences.i: The old woman had only pretended to be so kind; she was really a wicked witch who lay in wait for children, and had built the little bread house only to lure them to her.
sentences.n: „sich anstellen“ = to act, put on an appearance (separable). „auflauern“ + dative = to lie in wait for. „herbeilocken“ = to lure hither; „um … zu“ = in order to.

sentences.t: Wenn eins in ihre Gewalt kam, so machte sie es todt, kochte es und aß es, und das war ihr ein Festtag.
sentences.l: When one into her power came, so made she it dead, cooked it and ate it, and that was to-her a feast-day.
sentences.i: Whenever one fell into her power, she killed it, cooked it, and ate it, and that was a feast day for her.
sentences.n: „eins“ = 'one [child]' (neuter, since Kind is neuter; hence „es“ throughout). „todt machen“ (modern totmachen) = to kill. „das war ihr ein Festtag“: dative „ihr“ ('for her').

sentences.t: Die Hexen haben rothe Augen und können nicht weit sehen, aber sie haben eine feine Witterung, wie die Thiere, und merkens wenn Menschen heran kommen.
sentences.l: The witches have red eyes and can not far see, but they have a fine scent, like the animals, and notice-it when humans near come.
sentences.i: Witches have red eyes and cannot see far, but they have a keen sense of smell, like the animals, and notice when people come near.
sentences.n: „rothe“, „Thiere“ = 1857 spellings of „rote“, „Tiere“. „Witterung“ = (an animal's) scent, sense of smell. „merkens“ = „merken es“.

sentences.t: Als Hänsel und Grethel in ihre Nähe kamen, da lachte sie boshaft und sprach höhnisch „die habe ich, die sollen mir nicht wieder entwischen.“
sentences.l: When Hänsel and Grethel into her nearness came, there laughed she maliciously and spoke scornfully: “those have I, those shall to-me not again slip-away.”
sentences.i: When Hansel and Gretel came near her, she laughed maliciously and said with a sneer: “These I've got — they shan't slip away from me again.”
sentences.n: Fronting for emphasis: „die habe ich“ = 'these I have' (object first). „mir entwischen“ = to slip away from me (dative). „die sollen … nicht entwischen“ = 'they shall not escape'.

sentences.t: Früh Morgens ehe die Kinder erwacht waren, stand sie schon auf, und als sie beide so lieblich ruhen sah, mit den vollen rothen Backen, so murmelte sie vor sich hin „das wird ein guter Bissen werden.“
sentences.l: Early of-mornings, before the children awoken were, stood she already up, and when she both so sweetly rest saw, with the full red cheeks, so murmured she before herself along: “that will a good morsel become.”
sentences.i: Early in the morning, before the children had woken, she was already up, and when she saw the two of them resting so sweetly, with their full red cheeks, she muttered to herself: “That will make a tasty morsel.”
sentences.n: „vor sich hin murmeln“ = to mutter to oneself. „ruhen sah“: perception verb + bare infinitive ('saw [them] rest'). „das wird ein guter Bissen werden“ = future ('that will become a good morsel').

sentences.t: Da packte sie Hänsel mit ihrer dürren Hand und trug ihn in einen kleinen Stall und sperrte ihn mit einer Gitterthüre ein; er mochte schreien wie er wollte, es half ihm nichts.
sentences.l: There grabbed she Hänsel with her scrawny hand and carried him into a small stall and locked him with a lattice-door in; he might scream how he wanted, it helped him nothing.
sentences.i: She seized Hansel with her scrawny hand, carried him into a little stall, and shut him in behind a barred door; he might scream as he liked — it did him no good.
sentences.n: „einsperren“ = to shut/lock in (separable): „sperrte … ein“. „er mochte schreien wie er wollte“ = 'scream as he might' (concessive use of „mögen“). „es half ihm nichts“ = it was no use to him.

sentences.t: Dann gieng sie zur Grethel, rüttelte sie wach und rief „steh auf, Faullenzerin, trag Wasser und koch deinem Bruder etwas gutes, der sitzt draußen im Stall und soll fett werden.
sentences.l: Then went she to-the Grethel, shook her awake and called: “stand up, lazybones, carry water and cook to-your brother something good, he sits outside in-the stall and shall fat become.
sentences.i: Then she went to Gretel, shook her awake, and cried: “Get up, lazybones! Fetch water and cook something good for your brother; he's out there in the stall and is to be fattened up.
sentences.n: „zur Grethel“ (with article before a name) is colloquial/dialectal. „koch deinem Bruder etwas“: dative „deinem Bruder“ ('for your brother'). „soll fett werden“ = 'is to get fat' — „sollen“ expressing someone's intention for him.

sentences.t: Wenn er fett ist, so will ich ihn essen.“
sentences.l: When he fat is, so will I him eat.
sentences.i: When he's fat, I'll eat him.”

sentences.t: Grethel fieng an bitterlich zu weinen, aber es war alles vergeblich, sie mußte thun was die böse Hexe verlangte.
sentences.l: Grethel began bitterly to weep, but it was all in-vain, she must do what the evil witch demanded.
sentences.i: Gretel began to weep bitterly, but it was all in vain; she had to do what the wicked witch demanded.
sentences.n: „thun“ = 1857 spelling of „tun“ (to do). „es war alles vergeblich“ = it was all for nothing.

sentences.t: Nun ward dem armen Hänsel das beste Essen gekocht, aber Grethel bekam nichts als Krebsschalen.
sentences.l: Now was to-the poor Hänsel the best food cooked, but Grethel got nothing but crab-shells.
sentences.i: Now the best food was cooked for poor Hansel, but Gretel got nothing but crab shells.
sentences.n: „dem armen Hänsel“ is a dative ('for poor Hansel') with the passive „ward gekocht“. „nichts als“ = nothing but. „Krebsschalen“ = the empty shells of crayfish — scraps.

sentences.t: Jeden Morgen schlich die Alte zu dem Ställchen und rief „Hänsel, streck deine Finger heraus, damit ich fühle ob du bald fett bist.“
sentences.l: Every morning crept the old-one to the little-stall and called: “Hänsel, stretch your fingers out, so-that I feel whether you soon fat are.”
sentences.i: Every morning the old woman crept to the little stall and called: “Hansel, stick out your fingers, so I can feel whether you'll soon be fat.”
sentences.n: „herausstrecken“ = to stick/stretch out (separable). „ob“ (whether) sends its verb to the end („bist“).

sentences.t: Hänsel streckte ihr aber ein Knöchlein heraus, und die Alte, die trübe Augen hatte, konnte es nicht sehen, und meinte es wären Hänsels Finger, und verwunderte sich daß er gar nicht fett werden wollte.
sentences.l: Hänsel stretched to-her however a little-bone out, and the old-one, who dim eyes had, could it not see, and thought it were Hänsel's fingers, and wondered herself that he not-at-all fat become wanted.
sentences.i: But Hansel would stick out a little bone to her, and the old woman, whose eyes were dim, could not see it and thought it was Hansel's finger, and wondered why he would not get fat at all.
sentences.n: „Knöchlein“ = a little bone (diminutive). „meinte es wären …“: „wären“ subjunctive in reported thought. „sich verwundern“ = to be astonished (reflexive).

sentences.t: Als vier Wochen herum waren und Hänsel immer mager blieb, da übernahm sie die Ungeduld, und sie wollte nicht länger warten.
sentences.l: When four weeks around were and Hänsel always lean stayed, there overcame her the impatience, and she wanted not longer to-wait.
sentences.i: When four weeks had gone by and Hansel stayed as thin as ever, impatience got the better of her, and she would wait no longer.
sentences.n: „herum sein“ (of time) = to be up, over. „die Ungeduld übernahm sie“ = impatience overcame her (accusative „sie“).

sentences.t: „Heda, Grethel,“ rief sie dem Mädchen zu, „sei flink und trag Wasser: Hänsel mag fett oder mager sein, morgen will ich ihn schlachten und kochen.“
sentences.l: “Hey there, Grethel,” called she to-the girl, “be nimble and carry water: Hänsel may fat or lean be, tomorrow will I him slaughter and cook.”
sentences.i: “Hey there, Gretel,” she called to the girl, “be quick and fetch water; fat or thin, tomorrow I'll slaughter Hansel and cook him.”
sentences.n: „jemandem zurufen“ = to call out to someone (separable, dative „dem Mädchen“). „Hänsel mag fett oder mager sein“ = 'be Hansel fat or lean' (concessive „mögen“). The 1857 print here reads „dem“ where an earlier printing had „den“; „dem Mädchen“ (dative) is the correct reading.

sentences.t: Ach, wie jammerte das arme Schwesterchen, als es das Wasser tragen mußte, und wie flossen ihm die Thränen über die Backen herunter!
sentences.l: Ah, how lamented the poor little-sister, when it the water carry had-to, and how flowed to-it the tears over the cheeks down!
sentences.i: Oh, how the poor little sister wailed as she had to carry the water, and how the tears ran down over her cheeks!
sentences.n: „es“ / „ihm“ refer to „das Schwesterchen“ (neuter diminutive), so grammatically 'it', though Gretel is a girl. „die Thränen flossen ihm herunter“: dative of possession („ihm“) — 'her tears ran down'. „herunterfließen“ separable.

sentences.t: „Lieber Gott, hilf uns doch,“ rief sie aus, „hätten uns nur die wilden Thiere im Wald gefressen, so wären wir doch zusammen gestorben.“
sentences.l: “Dear God, help us do,” called she out, “had us only the wild beasts in-the forest devoured, so were we at-least together died.”
sentences.i: “Dear God, do help us,” she cried out; “if only the wild beasts in the forest had devoured us, at least we would have died together.”
sentences.n: „hätten … gefressen … so wären wir gestorben“ is a past unreal conditional (past subjunctive): 'had the beasts eaten us, we would have died' — a wish about how things might have gone. „ausrufen“ = to cry out. „fressen“ = to eat (of animals/devouring), harsher than „essen“.

sentences.t: „Spar nur dein Geblärre,“ sagte die Alte, „es hilft dir alles nichts.“
sentences.l: “Save just your bawling,” said the old-one, “it helps you all nothing.”
sentences.i: “Save your bawling,” said the old woman; “none of it will do you any good.”
sentences.n: „Geblärre“ = (contemptuous) blubbering, bawling. „es hilft dir alles nichts“ = 'none of it helps you at all'.

sentences.t: Früh Morgens mußte Grethel heraus, den Kessel mit Wasser aufhängen und Feuer anzünden.
sentences.l: Early of-mornings had Grethel out, the kettle with water to-hang-up and fire to-kindle.
sentences.i: Early in the morning Gretel had to go out, hang up the kettle of water, and light the fire.
sentences.n: „mußte … heraus“ = 'had to [go] out' (motion verb dropped after the modal). „aufhängen“, „anzünden“ both separable.

sentences.t: „Erst wollen wir backen“ sagte die Alte, „ich habe den Backofen schon eingeheizt und den Teig geknätet.“
sentences.l: “First want we to-bake,” said the old-one, “I have the bake-oven already heated and the dough kneaded.”
sentences.i: “First we'll do the baking,” said the old woman; “I've already heated the oven and kneaded the dough.”
sentences.n: „einheizen“ = to heat up (an oven), separable. „geknätet“ = old form of „geknetet“ (kneaded).

sentences.t: Sie stieß das arme Grethel hinaus zu dem Backofen, aus dem die Feuerflammen schon heraus schlugen.
sentences.l: She shoved the poor Grethel out to the bake-oven, out-of which the fire-flames already out struck.
sentences.i: She shoved poor Gretel out to the oven, out of which the flames were already leaping.
sentences.n: „hinausstoßen“ = to shove out (separable). „herausschlagen“ (of flames) = to leap/burst out.

sentences.t: „Kriech hinein,“ sagte die Hexe, „und sieh zu ob recht eingeheizt ist, damit wir das Brot hineinschießen können.“
sentences.l: “Crawl in,” said the witch, “and see to whether properly heated is, so-that we the bread in-shoot can.”
sentences.i: “Crawl in,” said the witch, “and see whether it's properly heated, so we can put the bread in.”
sentences.n: „hineinkriechen“ = to crawl in. „zusehen ob“ = to check whether. „hineinschießen“: to slide bread quickly into an oven (with a baker's peel).

sentences.t: Und wenn Grethel darin war, wollte sie den Ofen zumachen, und Grethel sollte darin braten, und dann wollte sies auch aufessen.
sentences.l: And when Grethel therein was, wanted she the oven to-close, and Grethel should therein roast, and then wanted she-it also up-eat.
sentences.i: And once Gretel was inside, she meant to shut the oven, so that Gretel would roast in it, and then she meant to eat her up too.
sentences.n: „zumachen“ = to close (separable). „darin“ = 'in it/there inside'. „sies“ = „sie es“.

sentences.t: Aber Grethel merkte was sie im Sinn hatte und sprach „ich weiß nicht wie ichs machen soll; wie komm ich da hinein?“
sentences.l: But Grethel noticed what she in-the mind had and spoke: “I know not how I-it do shall; how come I there in?”
sentences.i: But Gretel saw what she had in mind and said: “I don't know how to do it; how do I get in there?”
sentences.n: „etwas im Sinn haben“ = to have something in mind, be up to something. „ichs“ = „ich es“. „da hinein“ = in there.

sentences.t: „Dumme Gans,“ sagte die Alte, „die Öffnung ist groß genug, siehst du wohl, ich könnte selbst hinein,“ krappelte heran und steckte den Kopf in den Backofen.
sentences.l: “Stupid goose,” said the old-one, “the opening is big enough, see you well, I could myself in,” scrabbled up and stuck the head into the bake-oven.
sentences.i: “Stupid goose,” said the old woman, “the opening is big enough — see for yourself, I could get in myself,” and she scrabbled up and stuck her head into the oven.
sentences.n: „dumme Gans“ = 'silly goose', a common insult. „ich könnte selbst hinein“ = 'I could [get] in myself' (motion verb dropped after modal). „krappeln“ (dialectal) = to scrabble, clamber.

sentences.t: Da gab ihr Grethel einen Stoß daß sie weit hinein fuhr, machte die eiserne Thür zu und schob den Riegel vor.
sentences.l: There gave to-her Grethel a shove that she far in shot, made the iron door shut and pushed the bolt across.
sentences.i: Then Gretel gave her a shove that sent her right inside, slammed the iron door shut, and pushed the bolt across.
sentences.n: „jemandem einen Stoß geben“ = to give someone a push (dative „ihr“). „hineinfahren“ here = to shoot/plunge in. „zumachen“ and „vorschieben“ (to bolt) both separable: „machte … zu“, „schob … vor“.

sentences.t: Hu! da fieng sie an zu heulen, ganz grauselich; aber Grethel lief fort, und die gottlose Hexe mußte elendiglich verbrennen.
sentences.l: Ugh! there began she to howl, quite gruesomely; but Grethel ran away, and the godless witch had-to miserably burn-up.
sentences.i: Ugh! then she began to howl most horribly; but Gretel ran off, and the godless witch had to burn to death in misery.
sentences.n: „fortlaufen“ = to run away (separable). „elendiglich“ = an old, emphatic form of „elend“ (miserably). „verbrennen“ (intransitive) = to burn up, be consumed by fire.

sentences.t: Grethel aber lief schnurstracks zum Hänsel, öffnete sein Ställchen und rief „Hänsel, wir sind erlöst, die alte Hexe ist todt.“
sentences.l: Grethel however ran straightaway to-the Hänsel, opened his little-stall and called: “Hänsel, we are delivered, the old witch is dead.”
sentences.i: But Gretel ran straight to Hansel, opened his little stall, and cried: “Hansel, we're saved! The old witch is dead.”
sentences.n: „schnurstracks“ = straight away, directly. „erlöst“ = redeemed, delivered (past participle of „erlösen“). „todt“ = 1857 spelling of „tot“ (dead).

sentences.t: Da sprang Hänsel heraus, wie ein Vogel aus dem Käfig, wenn ihm die Thüre aufgemacht wird.
sentences.l: There sprang Hänsel out, like a bird out-of the cage, when to-it the door opened is.
sentences.i: Then Hansel sprang out, like a bird from its cage when the door is opened for it.
sentences.n: „herausspringen“ = to spring out (separable). „wenn ihm die Thüre aufgemacht wird“: passive („wird aufgemacht“) with dative „ihm“ ('when the door is opened for it').

sentences.t: Wie haben sie sich gefreut, sind sich um den Hals gefallen, sind herumgesprungen und haben sich geküßt!
sentences.l: How have they themselves rejoiced, are themselves about the neck fallen, are around-jumped and have themselves kissed!
sentences.i: How they rejoiced, threw their arms around each other's necks, jumped about, and kissed!
sentences.n: „jemandem um den Hals fallen“ = to fall on someone's neck, embrace. Motion verbs form the perfect with „sein“ (sind … gefallen, sind herumgesprungen), other verbs with „haben“. „sich“ here is reciprocal ('each other'). „geküßt“ = 1857 spelling of „geküsst“.

sentences.t: Und weil sie sich nicht mehr zu fürchten brauchten, so giengen sie in das Haus der Hexe hinein, da standen in allen Ecken Kasten mit Perlen und Edelsteinen.
sentences.l: And because they themselves no more to fear needed, so went they into the house of-the witch in, there stood in all corners chests with pearls and precious-stones.
sentences.i: And because they no longer needed to be afraid, they went into the witch's house, where in every corner stood chests full of pearls and jewels.
sentences.n: „sich fürchten“ = to be afraid (reflexive). „brauchen“ + „zu“ + infinitive = to need to (here negated: 'need no longer'). „hineingehen“ separable: „giengen … hinein“.

sentences.t: „Die sind noch besser als Kieselsteine“ sagte Hänsel und steckte in seine Taschen was hinein wollte, und Grethel sagte „ich will auch etwas mit nach Haus bringen“ und füllte sich sein Schürzchen voll.
sentences.l: “These are yet better than pebbles,” said Hänsel and stuck into his pockets what in wanted, and Grethel said: “I will also something with to home bring,” and filled herself its little-apron full.
sentences.i: “These are even better than pebbles,” said Hansel, and stuffed into his pockets whatever would go in; and Gretel said: “I'll take something home too,” and filled her little apron full.
sentences.n: „was hinein wollte“ = 'whatever would go in' (echoing the pebbles earlier). „sich etwas voll füllen“: „sich“ dative for one's own benefit. „sein Schürzchen“: „sein“ ('its') because „Schwesterchen/Mädchen“ is neuter — refers to Gretel.

sentences.t: „Aber jetzt wollen wir fort,“ sagte Hänsel, „damit wir aus dem Hexenwald herauskommen.“
sentences.l: “But now want we away,” said Hänsel, “so-that we out-of the witch-forest out-come.”
sentences.i: “But now let's be off,” said Hansel, “so we can get out of the witch's forest.”
sentences.n: „wir wollen fort“ = 'let's go' (motion verb dropped after modal). „herauskommen“ separable, verb-final after „damit“.

sentences.t: Als sie aber ein paar Stunden gegangen waren, gelangten sie an ein großes Wasser.
sentences.l: When they however a couple hours gone were, reached they at a great water.
sentences.i: But when they had walked a couple of hours, they came to a great stretch of water.
sentences.n: „an etwas gelangen“ = to reach, arrive at something. „ein großes Wasser“ = a large body of water (a lake or wide river).

sentences.t: „Wir können nicht hinüber,“ sprach Hänsel, „ich sehe keinen Steg und keine Brücke.“
sentences.l: “We can not over,” spoke Hänsel, “I see no footbridge and no bridge.”
sentences.i: “We can't get across,” said Hansel; “I see no plank and no bridge.”
sentences.n: „wir können nicht hinüber“ = 'we can't [get] across'. „Steg“ = a narrow footbridge or plank.

sentences.t: „Hier fährt auch kein Schiffchen,“ antwortete Grethel, „aber da schwimmt eine weiße Ente, wenn ich die bitte, so hilft sie uns hinüber.“
sentences.l: “Here goes also no little-ship,” answered Grethel, “but there swims a white duck, if I her ask, so helps she us over.”
sentences.i: “There's no little boat here either,” answered Gretel, “but a white duck is swimming there; if I ask her, she'll help us across.”
sentences.n: „fahren“ of a boat = to travel/ply. „die“ = 'her' (the duck), a demonstrative used as pronoun. „hinüberhelfen“ = to help across (separable, dative „uns“).

sentences.t: Da rief sie
sentences.l: Then called she
sentences.i: Then she called:

sentences.t:
„Entchen, Entchen,
da steht Grethel und Hänsel.
Kein Steg und keine Brücke,
nimm uns auf deinen weißen Rücken.“
sentences.l:
“Little-duck, little-duck,
there stand Grethel and Hänsel.
No footbridge and no bridge,
take us on your white back.”
sentences.i:
“Little duck, little duck,
here stand Gretel and Hansel.
No plank and no bridge —
take us on your white back.”
sentences.n: „Entchen“ = little duck (diminutive of Ente). The rhyme pairs Hänsel / (weißen) Rücken loosely by assonance; the couplet Brücke / Rücken carries the true rhyme.

sentences.t: Das Entchen kam auch heran, und Hänsel setzte sich auf und bat sein Schwesterchen sich zu ihm zu setzen.
sentences.l: The little-duck came indeed up, and Hänsel set himself on and asked his little-sister herself to him to set.
sentences.i: The little duck did come up, and Hansel sat himself on it and asked his little sister to sit beside him.
sentences.n: „heran kommen“ = to come up. „sich aufsetzen“ = to sit oneself on. „bat“ = past of „bitten“ (to ask, request).

sentences.t: „Nein,“ antwortete Grethel, „es wird dem Entchen zu schwer, es soll uns nach einander hinüber bringen.“
sentences.l: “No,” answered Grethel, “it becomes to-the little-duck too heavy, it shall us after one-another over bring.”
sentences.i: “No,” answered Gretel, “that would be too heavy for the little duck; it should carry us across one after the other.”
sentences.n: „es wird dem Entchen zu schwer“: dative „dem Entchen“ ('too heavy for the duck'). „nach einander“ = one after another. „hinüberbringen“ separable.

sentences.t: Das that das gute Thierchen, und als sie glücklich drüben waren und ein Weilchen fortgiengen, da kam ihnen der Wald immer bekannter und immer bekannter vor, und endlich erblickten sie von weitem ihres Vaters Haus.
sentences.l: That did the good little-animal, and when they happily over-there were and a little-while on-went, there came to-them the forest ever more-familiar and ever more-familiar, and finally caught-sight they from afar their father's house.
sentences.i: The good little creature did so, and when they were safely across and had gone on a little while, the forest looked more and more familiar to them, and at last they caught sight of their father's house in the distance.
sentences.n: „jemandem bekannt vorkommen“ = to seem familiar to someone (separable „kam … vor“, dative „ihnen“). „that“ / „Thierchen“ = 1857 spellings (tat, Tierchen). „von weitem“ = from afar.

sentences.t: Da fiengen sie an zu laufen, stürzten in die Stube hinein und fielen ihrem Vater um den Hals.
sentences.l: There began they to run, rushed into the parlor in and fell to-their father about the neck.
sentences.i: Then they began to run, burst into the room, and threw their arms around their father's neck.
sentences.n: „hineinstürzen“ = to rush in (separable). „jemandem um den Hals fallen“ = to fling one's arms around someone's neck (dative „ihrem Vater“).

sentences.t: Der Mann hatte keine frohe Stunde gehabt, seitdem er die Kinder im Walde gelassen hatte, die Frau aber war gestorben.
sentences.l: The man had no glad hour had, since he the children in-the forest left had, the woman however was died.
sentences.i: The man had not had one happy hour since he had left the children in the forest, but the wife had died.
sentences.n: „seitdem“ (since) sends its verb to the end („gelassen hatte“). „war gestorben“: „sterben“ (to die) forms its perfect with „sein“, not „haben“.

sentences.t: Grethel schüttete sein Schürzchen aus daß die Perlen und Edelsteine in der Stube herumsprangen, und Hänsel warf eine Handvoll nach der andern aus seiner Tasche dazu.
sentences.l: Grethel poured its little-apron out that the pearls and precious-stones in the parlor around-sprang, and Hänsel threw a handful after the other out-of his pocket to-it.
sentences.i: Gretel shook out her little apron, so that the pearls and jewels went bouncing about the room, and Hansel threw one handful after another out of his pocket to add to them.
sentences.n: „ausschütten“ = to pour/shake out (separable). „eine Handvoll nach der andern“ = one handful after another. „dazu“ = 'in addition, to it'.

sentences.t: Da hatten alle Sorgen ein Ende, und sie lebten in lauter Freude zusammen.
sentences.l: There had all worries an end, and they lived in nothing-but joy together.
sentences.i: Then all their cares were at an end, and they lived together in nothing but joy.
sentences.n: „in lauter Freude“ = in sheer/nothing-but joy (see „lauter“ earlier).

sentences.t: Mein Märchen ist aus, dort lauft eine Maus, wer sie fängt, darf sich eine große große Pelzkappe daraus machen.
sentences.l: My tale is out, there runs a mouse, who her catches, may himself a big big fur-cap out-of-it make.
sentences.i: My tale is done; there runs a mouse — whoever catches it may make himself a great big fur cap out of it.
sentences.n: A traditional rhyming tag closing the tale (aus / Maus). „mein Märchen ist aus“ = 'my tale is over'; „daraus“ = out of it (the mouse's fur).

glossary.a: A — the letter A (Wer A sagt muß auch B sagen: once you begin you must go on)
glossary.acht: die Acht — heed (Acht haben: to pay attention, take care)
glossary.ade: Ade — farewell, goodbye (archaic, from French à Dieu)
glossary.allen: allen — all (dat. pl. of all; in allen Ecken: in every corner)
glossary.aller: of all — in aller Eile: in all haste
glossary.alte: old — der Alte: the old man
glossary.am: = an dem, at the / on the
glossary.anbrach: anbrach — broke, dawned (anbrechen, of day; separable)
glossary.anbrechendem: anbrechendem — breaking, dawning (pres. part. of anbrechen; bei anbrechendem Tag: as day breaks)
glossary.angehört: angehört — listened to, overheard (mit anhören; separable)
glossary.angemacht: angemacht — lit, made (of a fire; anmachen; separable)
glossary.angestellt: angestellt — put on an act (sich anstellen: to feign, act; separable)
glossary.ans: = an das, to the / at the
glossary.aufgetragen: aufgetragen — served up (auftragen, of food; separable)
glossary.auflauerte: auflauerte — lay in wait for (auflauern + dat.; separable)
glossary.b: B — the letter B (Wer A sagt muß auch B sagen)
glossary.batzen: der Batzen — an old small silver coin
glossary.bei: at, with, by — bei der Hand: by the hand
glossary.beißen: beißen — to bite (zu beißen und zu brechen: to eat)
glossary.bekannter: bekannter — more familiar (bekannt, comparative; bekannt vorkommen: to seem familiar)
glossary.berg: der Berg — mountain (einen Berg hoch: a mountain high)
glossary.bette: das Bett — bed (dat. Bette, old -e ending)
glossary.bettlein: das Bettlein — little bed (diminutive of Bett)
glossary.bitte: bitte — ask, beg (bitten; wenn ich die bitte: if I ask her)
glossary.bleibt: bleibt — stay(s), remain(s) (bleiben; also imperative pl.)
glossary.brauchten: brauchten — needed (brauchen; brauchen + zu: to need to)
glossary.brechen: brechen — to break (zu beißen und zu brechen: to eat)
glossary.brothäuslein: das Brothäuslein — little house of bread
glossary.bröcklein: das Bröcklein — little crumb (diminutive of Brocken)
glossary.bübchen: das Bübchen — little boy (diminutive of Bube)
glossary.dache: das Dach — roof (dat. Dache, old -e ending)
glossary.daran: daran — at/on it (knupern daran: to nibble at it)
glossary.daraus: daraus — out of it, from it
glossary.davon: davon — of it, from it, away
glossary.deiner: of you (genitive of du — uns deiner schämen: be ashamed of you)
glossary.der: the (masculine); who, that; of the (feminine dative/genitive)
glossary.dicksten: dicksten — thickest (dick, superlative; wo er am dicksten ist)
glossary.dir: to you, for you (dative of du)
glossary.doch: doch — yet, after all (flavoring particle: insistence)
glossary.dran: dran — = daran, to/at it (sich dran machen: to set to it)
glossary.drüben: drüben — over there, on the other side
glossary.edelsteinen: die Edelsteine — precious stones, jewels (der Edelstein; dat. pl.)
glossary.ein: a, an; one; in (separable prefix, as in herein)
glossary.einander: einander — one another (nach einander: one after another)
glossary.einer: one, someone; (dative feminine) einer jeden: of each one
glossary.eingeheizt: eingeheizt — heated up (einheizen, of an oven; separable)
glossary.einmal: once; (after imperative) just — seht einmal: just look
glossary.entchen: das Entchen — little duck (diminutive of Ente)
glossary.entwischen: entwischen — to slip away, escape (+ dat.)
glossary.erblickten: erblickten — caught sight of (erblicken, past pl.)
glossary.erschracken: erschracken — took fright, were startled (erschrecken, intrans., past pl.)
glossary.es: it — also ‘she’ throughout, referring to das Mädchen (neuter)
glossary.euch: you (old respectful address, like French vous)
glossary.eßts: eßts — = eßt es, eat it (essen, imperative pl. + es)
glossary.fallen: fallen — to fall (fallen lassen: to drop)
glossary.felde: das Feld — field (dat. Felde, old -e ending)
glossary.fest: das Fest — festival, feast; fest (adj.): firm, tight
glossary.feuerflammen: die Feuerflammen — flames of fire (die Feuerflamme)
glossary.fiels: fiels — = fiel es (fallen; es fiel ihm schwer aufs Herz: it weighed on him)
glossary.fieng: fieng — began (anfangen; 1857 spelling of fing; separable)
glossary.fortgiengen: fortgiengen — went on, went away (fortgehen; 1857 spelling; separable)
glossary.freude: die Freude — joy (in lauter Freude: in nothing but joy)
